meta.reconciliation_note: Backlog re-shaped 2026-05-28 from three inputs: external adversarial review (~/github/scratch/eval.md), internal repo-health 2026-05-28 (.work/reviews/repo-health-2026-05-28/report.md), and the existential alternatives.md draft (docs/alternatives.md). Backlog ordered top-down by Phase-1 priority. See PR chore/reconcile-phase-1 for rationale.

backlog.title: chore: Phase 1 = discipline-restoration (tracker)
backlog.description: Repo-health 2026-05-28 (BLOCKING #4 goal-displacement, #5 validation-theatre, existential-challenger) — ~4650 lines of process artefacts (ADRs, SKILL, agents, commands, references) vs ~4145 lines of Python + tests, and one shipped capability (the gate). The product had become the process. `.work/reviews/` and `.work/insights/` held only README/.gitkeep before the 2026-05-28 review run; the 'every agent output is validated' loop had never closed with a saved finding. This is a governance fact, not a single fixable defect. It is captured here as a tracker so the rest of Phase 1 (everything below) can be measured against it: BLOCKING fixes that produce running code, governance closures that bring ADRs into line with implementation, README that leads with the gate, and the workstream model that makes 'one shipped capability per workstream' the unit of progress. This item closes only when every other Phase-1 item closes.
acceptance_criteria: Every other Phase-1 item is closed before this one.

Repo-health re-run after Phase 1 close shows the goal-displacement and validation-theatre findings resolved (process-to-code ratio improved or the surplus process retired; reviews directory contains at least one real run produced inside Phase 1).
.work/reviews/ contains at least the 2026-05-28 run plus the Phase-1-close existential cycle.
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: feat: state-backend migration script (local-file -> github-issues)
backlog.description: Per ADR-022 and the WORK-0007 audit, build a one-shot migration script that moves an existing .work/work.json backlog to GitHub Issues. Mirrors the existing install.py migration registry pattern (idempotent, archival, explicit). Invocation: install.py --migrate-state-backend github-issues (or a separate skill/hedl/scripts/migrate_state_backend.py — operator's call during implementation). Should be reversible in the sense that the archived local-file state can be restored, even though the resulting GitHub state cannot be cleanly auto-rolled-back.
acceptance_criteria: Script reads .work/work.json backlog, active, completed.

For each item, creates a GitHub issue via gh api with: title formatted 'WORK-XXXX: <title>' (preserves the historical ID for cross-references in commits/ADRs), body containing the description + acceptance criteria, labels mapping to change_class / phase / workstream / priority_band per the WORK-0007 scheme.
Records the mapping (issue-number ↔ WORK-XXXX) under .work/archive/<timestamp>/state-backend-migration.json.
Archives the pre-migration .work/work.json to .work/archive/<timestamp>/work.json.pre-github-issues.

Updates hedl.toml [state] backend = github-issues and any backend-specific configuration (label_prefix, repo, under [state_backend.github-issues]) per ADR-022 (state_backend lives in hedl.toml, not context.json — WORK-0024).
Empties .work/work.json (or removes it; operator's call — preserve backwards-compat reads if removal breaks the gate).
Idempotent: re-running on a repo already on github-issues backend is a no-op with a clear message.
Tests cover: round-trip mapping, idempotency, label correctness, archive contents.
am_i_done.py passes after migration (including gate read-paths via the github-issues backend).

Harden the gate read path (WORK-0007 audit follow-up): scope the github-issues read to Hedl-managed issues via the `hedl:work` label rather than title-matching alone (closes the title-injection / non-Hedl-collision trust gap), and paginate so the read is complete rather than failing at the _GITHUB_ISSUE_READ_LIMIT cap. Update skill/hedl/references/backends.md 'Known limitations' as each is resolved.
Migration applies the `hedl:work` label (and the WORK-0007 label scheme) to every created issue so the label-scoped read works from day one.
The stale-ID check is skipped or handled cleanly when the github backend has zero open WORK-issues (mirror the local-file no-work.json skip), and `gh` stderr is not leaked verbatim into gate output.
backlog.note: WORK-0007 audit (2026-05-30) folded the github-issues read-path hardening into this item: hedl:work label-scoped + paginated reads, label application on migration, and the empty-set/stderr handling. See WORK-0059 for the separate multi-operator (assignee/claim) layer.

backlog.title: docs: README and positioning updates (lead with gate; invisible qualifier; iteration-layer framing)
backlog.description: Update README and docs/getting-started.md to reflect the architectural story established in this ADR series and confirmed by the 2026-05-28 alternatives.md draft. Three threads: (a) lead with the gate (ADR-011, corroborated by the external review and the alternatives.md disqualifier story — the deterministic completion gate is the genuine differentiator), (b) Hedl-as-iteration-layer framing per ADR-022 (Hedl plugs into PM systems; doesn't replace them), (c) explicit 'Use Hedl if...' qualifier section per ADR-023 including the invisible-mode case. Honesty-over-marketing per ADR-010: no percentage-gain claims, no comparative superlatives. The alternatives.md stub already exists (committed in PR #10); this work uses it as the source for the disqualifier wording rather than re-creating it.
acceptance_criteria: README opens with the gate as the headline capability (ADR-011 lead-with-gate), citing alternatives.md as the disqualifier source.
README contains a 'Use Hedl if...' qualifier section including the invisible-mode case from ADR-023.
README and getting-started.md frame Hedl as an iteration layer above PM systems, not a replacement.
Cross-link from README to docs/alternatives.md and from getting-started to the disqualifier section.
No marketing claims (no percentages, no 'X% faster', no superlatives) survive review.
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: docs: terminology audit — align surviving recursive-'workstream' usages to the canonical four-category meaning (post-WORK-0027)
backlog.description: Per WORK-0027, 'workstream' canonically means the four-category work classification (WS-PLAN/REQ/TECH/ARCH; standards.md), and ADR-019's recursive-container primitive is superseded/deferred (WORK-0010). This audit sweeps the surviving shipped usages that still use 'workstream' in the recursive/container sense and brings them into line: ADR-020, ADR-021, ADR-031, docs/alternatives.md, docs/templates/bootstrap-adopter.md (and any WORK-0010/0011 prose). Each either aligns to the canonical meaning or is explicitly annotated as referencing the deferred recursive model (gated on WORK-0010), so no shipped doc presents the recursive primitive as the current meaning of 'workstream'. Also review 'phase' / 'work item' usage for consistency. Add a glossary entry if helpful. Depends on WORK-0027 (done) — and on the /phase-complete decision on whether the recursive cluster (WORK-0010, ADR-031) is kept-deferred or culled, which determines whether surviving references get annotated or removed.
acceptance_criteria: Surviving 'workstream' usages in the recursive/container sense (ADR-020, ADR-021, ADR-031, docs/alternatives.md, docs/templates/bootstrap-adopter.md) are each reviewed and either aligned to the canonical four-category meaning or explicitly annotated as referencing the deferred recursive model (WORK-0010).
No shipped doc presents the recursive primitive as the current meaning of 'workstream'; references to 'phase' / 'work item' are reviewed for consistency with the canonical model.
A glossary entry (standards.md or a new terminology.md) records the canonical meaning and the name reserved for the deferred recursive model.
am_i_done.py passes.
backlog.reframe: Retargeted 2026-05-30 by WORK-0027. Originally 'workstream replaces phase/work-item where ADR-019 specifies' — i.e. it assumed ADR-019's recursive model was canonical and would replace 'work item'/'phase'. WORK-0027 reversed that (four-category meaning canonical; ADR-019 superseded), so the audit's job is now the opposite: sweep the surviving recursive-sense usages and align/annotate them, rather than migrate everything to the recursive primitive.

backlog.title: feat: implement recursive workstream data model + schema migration
backlog.description: Per ADR-019, `.work/work.json` migrates from flat active/backlog/completed arrays to a recursive tree of workstreams. A leaf-level workstream = today's work item; a top-level workstream = today's phase. Bump `schema_version` and add a migration in install.py's registry that converts existing flat state to the recursive shape idempotently (with archival per the existing migration pattern). Solo + flat usage must remain functionally unchanged — leaf-only workstreams are the default for lightweight tier. Recursive composition is opt-in. Depends on WORK-0027 (canonical workstream definition) being settled. Forward-compatibility (ADR-031, Proposed-deferred): design the schema so dependency-edge fields (depends_on / parallel / conflicts_with) can be added without a further migration when ADR-031 is activated — do not foreclose them, but do not build them now (ADR-031 is gated on this item landing + real demand).
acceptance_criteria: `.work/work.json` schema supports recursive workstreams (children: [workstream...]); flat work items remain valid as leaf workstreams.
`schema_version` increments; install.py --migrate converts existing flat state to recursive shape idempotently.
Pre-migration state is archived to `.work/archive/<timestamp>/` (mirroring existing archival pattern).
Per ADR-021 forward-compatibility: workstream IDs are globally unique enough to survive future cross-repo references (UUIDs or composite keys).
Lightweight-tier solo workflow is functionally unchanged.
am_i_done.py passes including all existing tests on the new shape.
Tests cover: leaf workstream (flat), nested workstreams (epic-of-stories), tier-graded behaviour, migration round-trip.
backlog.note: Data-model-adoption threshold (dogfood 2026-05-29, N=2 adopters): the recursive model's benefit exceeds prose-template cost only when concurrently-active workstreams exceed ~5-7, OR >1 concurrent operator needs shared machine-readable state, OR a parallel-bootstrap-under-coordination-strain repeats. Below that, prose templates (docs/templates/bootstrap-adopter.md) are the right tool. See .work/insights/dogfood-workstream-spawn-adopters-2026-05-29.md.

backlog.title: feat: ship existential-review and problem-statement workstream templates
backlog.description: Per ADR-020, two prose-form reference templates ship in `skill/hedl/templates/workstreams/`. existential-review: iteration 1 = identify capability + competitors; iterations 2..N = investigate each competitor; final iteration = report and culling decision (consumed by ADR-012's managed competitive lifecycle). problem-statement: iteration 1 = broad problem + objective; iterations 2..N = discovery, planning, refinement; final iteration = deliverables (may spawn child workstreams). Workstream creation flow consults the template at start; the agent follows the iteration pattern. Adopter-authored templates live alongside the shipped ones; path configurable via hedl.toml. Templates are prose-form for v0.2; executable templates deferred. The first concrete use of existential-review template is the workstream that produced the 2026-05-28 alternatives.md.
acceptance_criteria: `skill/hedl/templates/workstreams/existential-review.md` exists with the iteration pattern described above.
`skill/hedl/templates/workstreams/problem-statement.md` exists with the iteration pattern described above.
Workstream creation flow in references/commands.md describes consulting the template at start.
hedl.toml supports a `[workstreams] templates_path` override.

First concrete use: existential-review runs on Hedl itself, producing docs/alternatives.md (already committed in PR #10 — reference it as the worked example); problem-statement is used to bootstrap Lume.
am_i_done.py passes.
backlog.note: When this ships workstream templates in data-form (ADR-020), fold in / supersede the prose docs/templates/bootstrap-adopter.md (authored 2026-05-29 from the Lume/Wyrd bootstraps). See .work/insights/dogfood-workstream-spawn-adopters-2026-05-29.md.

backlog.title: fix: install.py path resolves in consumer repos
backlog.description: SKILL.md routes install operations to `python3 skill/hedl/scripts/install.py`. In this (self-hosted) repo `skill/hedl/` is at the root so the path resolves. In a consumer repo, the skill is symlinked to `.claude/skills/hedl/` and `install.py` is NOT projected by any tier in `tiers.json`. An agent in an installed consumer repo following SKILL.md verbatim will hit 'file not found' for every install operation: --status, --doctor, --tier change, --migrate. Fix options (TBD at implementation time): (a) project `install.py` under one of the tiers, (b) re-route SKILL.md to the symlinked skill location, (c) ship a thin shim that resolves the skill path. This finding came from an external review with consumer context; a self-hosted /repo-health run cannot detect it because the failing condition does not exist in this repo.
acceptance_criteria: Install management instructions in SKILL.md resolve and execute successfully in an installed consumer repo (any tier).
A test simulates the consumer layout (skill symlinked to .claude/skills/hedl/) and exercises --status, --doctor, --tier change, and --migrate without 'file not found'.
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: feat: ship the external adversarial review kit (consistency + report contract)
backlog.description: Operationalise the external adversarial review (ADR-028, Proposed) so every run is consistent and its output is reconcilable like /repo-health. Today the review depends on the operator pasting the prompt from memory and runs ad hoc. Ship the canonical prompt as a versioned skill artifact and define the output contract. Captured 2026-05-28 from the mid-Phase-1 dogfood (the operator shared the actual external-review prompt) and the WORK-0001/0002/0003 evidence that external review finds consumer-context defects a self-hosted /repo-health structurally cannot (learning #7). Independence is the point: the review runs OUTSIDE Hedl's context (separate Claude instance, sandbox clone, scripts never executed) — the in-context operator/agent must not be the reviewer. Building the kit does NOT auto-Accept ADR-028's cadence; ADR-028 stays Proposed/gated on reviews #2 and #3.
acceptance_criteria: skill/hedl/references/external-review.md ships the canonical prompt: clone-to-sandbox + never-run-scripts protocol; the dimensions (quality, security, utility, accuracy, maintenance, supply-chain) weighed against actual Claude Code utility per the claude_code_docs CURRENT AT REVIEW TIME, with the docs version / retrieval-date recorded in the report so the assessment stays reviewable later; run by an independent Claude instance outside Hedl's context.

Output contract documented and reuses existing machinery: each adversarial lens emits .work/reviews/external-<date>/<lens>.json using the same per-finding schema as /repo-health ({severity, category, finding, evidence, recommendation} plus the optional detail field /repo-health already uses; additional optional fields allowed). A synthesised report.md (in-repo) conforms to the existing review-report markdown schema; no new validator is added (the gate's schemas check validates external reports).

Canonical artifacts are defined unambiguously: report.md (under .work/reviews/external-<date>/) is the in-repo, schema-conforming, backlog-reconcilable record; eval.md (the portable 1-page operator summary, e.g. ~/github/scratch/eval.md) is the human-facing digest derived from the same findings. The kit states both filenames and their distinct roles.

An independent alternatives/disqualifiers pass is part of the review, emitted as .work/reviews/external-<date>/alternatives.md and kept SEPARATE from docs/alternatives.md (merging into the repo's own doc is the operator's reconciliation decision, preserving independence). Feeds ADR-011 / ADR-012.

The clone/handoff seam is documented: the external instance cannot write into this repo, so it produces eval.md + report.md + the per-lens JSON in its sandbox and the operator drops report.md + JSON into .work/reviews/external-<date>/ and reconciles into the backlog (as the 2026-05-28 review was, per meta.reconciliation_note).
README/tiers/docs reference the kit and any agent/command counts stay consistent (skill-meta + docs-index checks pass).
Relationship to ADR-028 recorded: this is its implementation; ADR-028 remains Proposed until reviews #2/#3 clear its evidence gate.
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: docs: retrospective Prior-art audit of ADR-001 through ADR-016
backlog.description: ADR-017 requires every ADR to include a `Prior art` section that names overlapping standards/practices and (if overlap exists) justifies Hedl's version. ADR-001 through ADR-016 were written before ADR-017 and do not include this section. Audit each one and add a Prior art section: name the overlapping practice (or state none), answer the three justification questions where overlap exists. Several of the early ADRs apply standard patterns (e.g. ADR-002 tiered adoption, ADR-007 naming convention, ADR-009 opinionated defaults) and should explicitly acknowledge this — being honest about not inventing is the point. ADR-017 does not block on this audit; ADR-001..016 remain Accepted but become more defensible with annotation. Deferred to Phase 2 because it is parallel-able and not on the critical path of any Phase-1 BLOCKING or governance closure.
acceptance_criteria: Each of ADR-001 through ADR-016 includes a `Prior art` section that either names overlapping standards/practices or states that no relevant prior art exists.

For ADRs with overlap: the section answers (a) what is genuinely different about Hedl's version, (b) why the difference is worth the cost, (c) what would have to change for Hedl to delegate to the existing thing.
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: feat: lightweight-tier multi-operator support (assignees, claim/release, flock)
backlog.description: Per ADR-018, lightweight tier must support multi-operator coordination via local `.work/` mechanisms. Add an `assignee` field to work items, change `meta.active_item` to `meta.active_items` keyed by assignee, wrap `.work/work.json` writes with `flock` (mirror the budget_manager.py pattern), and add a claim/release lifecycle (open → claimed → in_progress → released → completed). Bump `schema_version` and add a migration. Solo adoption must be unchanged — assignee defaults to the current operator. Deferred to Phase 2 because it depends on the recursive workstream schema (WORK-0010) landing first.
acceptance_criteria: Work items carry an `assignee` field (defaults to current git user if unset).
`meta.active_items` keyed by assignee replaces singular `meta.active_item`; backward-compat read is supported.
Concurrent writes to `.work/work.json` are flock-serialised (mirroring budget_manager).
Claim/release lifecycle: `/claim <id>` and `/release <id>` flows defined in references/commands.md.
`schema_version` increments; install.py migration handles the upgrade idempotently with state archival.
Solo operator workflow is functionally unchanged (no new prompts, no extra steps).
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: feat: tier-transition coordination-state migrations in install.py

backlog.title: feat: install.py --invisible / --make-visible / --skill-from flags
backlog.description: Per ADR-023, implement Hedl's invisible mode. Three install.py additions. (1) --invisible <tier> flag: performs a normal tier install AND inserts a delimited Hedl block into .gitignore covering .work/, .hedl-tier, .github/ projections, .claude/ projections, hedl.toml. (2) --make-visible flag: removes the gitignore block (additive-only, never touches other rules); state of files on disk is unchanged. (3) --skill-from <path> flag: installs from a skill at an arbitrary path (e.g., ~/tools/hedl/skill/hedl/) so the operator's project doesn't need the skill source committed. The CI-gate-doesn't-run-in-invisible-mode tradeoff is surfaced in install output and docs.
acceptance_criteria: `install.py --invisible <tier>` installs the tier AND adds a delimited `# >>> hedl >>>` ... `# <<< hedl <<<` block to .gitignore covering all Hedl artifacts.
`install.py --make-visible` removes the block (additive-only management; never touches other .gitignore rules).
`install.py --skill-from <path>` allows installation from a skill at an arbitrary path; default remains the current behaviour.
Invisible-mode install output explicitly warns that team CI gate does not run on the operator's PRs.
Tests cover: invisible install, make-visible round-trip, gitignore block idempotency, skill-from with both absolute and relative paths.
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: feat: persona contract + change_class auto-selection (deferred — demand-gated)
backlog.description: Backlog capture of the persona idea from the 2026-05-28 design discussion. Proposed as ADR-032 but the manual existential review FAILed it: a persona contract + auto-selection table with no orchestrator to consume them is process-without-product (the validation-theatre pattern repo-health rated BLOCKING), and its 'differentiators' (gate / deterministic dispatch / journey) are existing machinery re-pointed at a 'persona' noun that is relabelled .claude/agents/ frontmatter. Held here, NOT as a Proposed ADR, until there is demonstrated build-side demand for multi-persona work. The idea: a persona contract (frontmatter like .claude/agents/: name, mandate, tools, model, engages_for:[change_class...]) projected from skill/hedl/personas/, deterministic change_class -> persona auto-selection, with the existing gate + adversarial review + journey as the validation substrate; orchestration delegated, not built.
acceptance_criteria: Activated only on demonstrated build-side demand for multi-persona work (e.g. surfaced while bootstrapping Lume) — not built speculatively.

Before building, evaluate delegating orchestration to CrewAI / AutoGen / Claude subagents vs a Hedl-native contract, per ADR-012 (managed competitive lifecycle); prefer delegation + gate/journey wrap if it suffices.
If built: persona contract mirrors the .claude/agents/ frontmatter schema; deterministic change_class auto-selection (no inference, ADR-003); gate + journey applied per persona; depends on WORK-0010.
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: fix: gate PR-template check exempts bot-authored (Dependabot) PRs
backlog.description: Observed 2026-05-29 while triaging Dependabot PRs #39/#41/#42: am_i_done's --pr template check (check_pr_template.py) fails on every Dependabot PR because the bot's body does not match PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md. The operator had to hand-rewrite each PR body to satisfy Summary/Work item/Type/Changes/Validation and then `gh pr update-branch` to re-run the gate before the bot PR could go green and merge — three times in one session. Remove the friction: the template check should exempt (or minimally auto-satisfy) PRs authored by the Dependabot app. Security note (this is gate code, ADR-003 / RCE-area discipline): the exemption MUST key off the verified PR author login being the Dependabot bot (e.g. `gh pr view --json author`), never a body marker or branch-name string, both of which a malicious PR could spoof to skip template enforcement.
acceptance_criteria: am_i_done.py --pr N template check skips (or auto-passes) when the PR author is the Dependabot app, verified via the GitHub author login (gh pr view --json author), not body text or branch name.
Human-authored PRs remain fully enforced: an incomplete human PR body still FAILs the template check.
Regression test: a Dependabot-authored PR context passes the template check with an unmodified bot body; a human PR with a placeholder/empty body still fails. Test fails before the fix, passes after.
Behaviour documented where the template check is described (pr-ready.md / references/commands.md or check_pr_template docstring).
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: feat: install.py --copy mode detects and warns on local drift to projected files
backlog.description: Lume bootstrap 2026-05-29: install.py --copy (the recommended adopter mode) silently overwrites any local edits the operator made to projected files, no warning. _project_one(): if target bytes differ from source, shutil.copy2 runs and returns 'updated' — no user-file-modified detection, confirmation, or backup. Affected: .claude/agents/*, .claude/commands/*, .claude/settings.json, .claude/startup.sh, .github/scripts/*, .github/workflows/*, .github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md, requirements-ci.txt, docs/spec/* (33 files at team tier). A data-loss class of surprise: an adopter who customised .claude/settings.json loses it on the next --copy install.
acceptance_criteria: For each projection, install.py records a content hash of what was last projected (extend .hedl-tier, or add a .hedl-manifest.json sibling).
On re-install, if target_bytes != source_bytes AND target_bytes != last_projected_hash, treat as user drift: log 'drift detected' and skip by default.
New flag --force-overwrite-drift opts into the current behaviour.
--dry-run lists drifted files explicitly so the operator can decide.
--doctor reports drifted files.
Test added: clean re-install (no drift), single-file drift detected and preserved, --force-overwrite-drift behaves as today.
docs/upgrading.md (WORK-0044) explains the drift-detection contract.
am_i_done.py passes.
backlog.note: Candidate sub-task of WORK-0050 (manifest projection ADR); superseded by it IF that ADR is accepted, otherwise stands alone.

backlog.title: docs: write docs/upgrading.md covering the adopter update workflow

backlog.title: feat(arch): installer projection model — adopt a tracked manifest (.hedl-manifest.json) as the Hedl<->adopter contract
backlog.description:
ADR-CLASS (Proposed ADR before child work can be specified). MORATORIUM CAVEAT: forced by the Lume bootstrap 2026-05-29 surface — six independent findings collapse to one root cause. Operator decides whether to land the Proposed ADR now or defer the cluster to /phase-complete; if deferred, Lume hand-patches each gap as it surfaces.

ROOT: install.py treats projection as a one-shot copy (read tiers.json, walk projections, write/skip, exit) with NO durable record of what was projected, when, from which Hedl source. This produces a family of asymmetric failures observed live during Lume bootstrap: (1=.gitignore/WORK-0042) can't ship adopter-protection files with a recorded reason; (2=drift/WORK-0043) silently overwrites adopter edits — no last-projected state to diff; (6) workflow projections carry preconditions (pyproject.toml + uv.lock) the installer can't audit; (7=.pymarkdown.json) config the outputs depend on but the installer doesn't ship; (8) docs/spec/ files create a downstream README docs-index obligation the installer doesn't track; (9) same-tier re-install can't detect files Hedl has since removed (orphans). All six share one gap: installer state lives only in tiers.json (Hedl-side) + touched paths (adopter-side); nothing records the RELATIONSHIP across versions.

PROPOSED ABSTRACTION: replace minimal .hedl-tier with a tracked-in-git .hedl-manifest.json the installer writes on every action — {schema_version, tier, hedl_version, last_installed_at, projections:{<target>:{source, source_hash, kind:projection|init|starter, ...}}}. Every install/upgrade reads the prior manifest, diffs vs current tiers.json, reconciles. Adopters commit it (multi-operator per ADR-018 sees the same state). Post-manifest each finding becomes: gitignore=append-or-create projection recorded as Hedl-managed block; drift=hash-diff vs manifest source_hash, skip-by-default + --force-overwrite-drift; pyproject/.pymarkdown=starter projections on_exists:skip; docs-index=starter README projection OR docs-index reads manifest to exempt unmodified starter files; orphans=previous_paths - current_paths, log + --remove-orphans (file-deletion blast radius => not auto).

EXISTENTIAL CHALLENGE (ADR-017): prior art is mature — Homebrew install records, apt/dpkg .list, Helm releases, Terraform state, npm package-lock. Risk = over-engineering (Hedl is a workflow scaffold, not a package manager). Challenge: (1) does it already exist (yes, compare the five); (2) manifest vs fixing each finding bespoke — list the bespoke code each individual fix adds and compare; (3) replace .hedl-tier vs compose alongside; (4) migration for existing adopters (synthesize v1 manifest from tiers.json + current source hashes, idempotent, one-time); (5) does committing it leak Hedl version vs invisible-mode (ADR-023).
acceptance_criteria: ADR phase: Proposed ADR-NNNN drafted with prior-art audit (Homebrew, apt/dpkg, Helm, Terraform, npm lockfile + any others); existential-challenger pass (now enforced via WORK-0005); operator decision recorded — ACCEPT (manifest adopted), REJECT (each child finding fixed individually as bespoke logic), or DEFER (document gaps, revisit).

If ACCEPTED: schema authored (skill/hedl/schemas/ or references/manifest-schema.md MVP); migration from .hedl-tier-only designed; all six child findings (Prompts 1/2/6/7/8/9 = WORK-0042, WORK-0043, + 6/7/8/9) re-scoped to their post-manifest form as sub-tasks.
If REJECTED: each of Prompts 1/2/6/7/8/9 becomes a standalone current-architecture-bound work item.
am_i_done.py passes (ADR PR is docs-only).

POST-ADR IMPLEMENTATION (if accepted): install.py reads prior manifest before action, computes create/update/orphan/starter sets, behaves per kind annotations; --doctor reports manifest schema version + projection health + orphan list; --status reports manifest summary; .hedl-tier->.hedl-manifest.json migration automatic on first re-install; integration test (fresh install -> remove a projection from tiers.json -> re-install detects + removes orphan under --remove-orphans); docs/upgrading.md (WORK-0044) updated to lead with manifest-based upgrade.

backlog.title: feat(arch): GitHub Projects (v2) as a presentation/organization surface alongside the github-issues state backend
backlog.description:
ADR-CLASS (Proposed ADR before implementation). MORATORIUM CAVEAT: operator decides land-now vs defer-to-/phase-complete. Escalates because ADR-022 (PM-system pluggability) assumed GitHub Issues was the right GitHub primitive, and that assumption deserves re-examination BEFORE more github-issues-backend code lands (WORK-0007/0032/0033).

ROOT QUESTION: ADR-022 has Hedl iterate on top of a PM system (PM = source of truth); the github-issues backend is in flight. GitHub also offers Projects v2 (boards/tables with custom fields: status, iteration, priority, workstream) organizing Issues/PRs/drafts at higher altitude — many adopter teams already use them. Hedl has no model for them. Options: (A) Projects-as-presentation-layer over github-issues (Issue stays source-of-truth; Project provides visualization + fields Hedl reads/writes from work-item state); (B) Projects-as-separate-backend (state_backend=github-projects; project items are the store); (C) Issues+Projects hybrid (one Issue + one linked Project item, bidirectional sync — best UX, most code); (D) don't support (document why; Issues stays state-of-record, adopters maintain Project view manually).

Why ADR-class: mis-aligning Hedl's model with how adopter teams organize work in GitHub is friction; interacts with WORK-0007 audit scope, WORK-0032 migration target, ADR-018 multi-operator (Projects' assignee model differs), WORK-0012 adapter contract; hardening github-issues now without resolving this risks duplicated effort or an Issues-only abstraction Projects must retrofit around.

EXISTENTIAL CHALLENGE (ADR-017): compare GitHub native Project automation (Hedl should CONSUME Project state, not recreate the issue-closed->card-moves loop), Linear/Jira board+issue models (github-projects is the GitHub-flavoured version ADR-022 anticipated), existing Projects integrations (project-actions etc., which invert direction). Challenge: (1) prior art; (2) is Issues even the right primitive or should Hedl have started with Projects (Issues are repo-tied; Projects span repos — relates ADR-021 multi-repo deferred); (3) Projects v2 is GraphQL-only — accidental complexity vs the stdlib-only contract (test_stdlib_only.py); use `gh api graphql` to avoid a new dep; (4) adopter benefit large enough for a 2nd backend; (5) effect on ADR-018 multi-operator assignee semantics.

RECOMMENDATION (subject to challenge): (A) Projects-as-presentation-layer, narrow — Issues remain state-of-record (preserves WORK-0007/0032/0033 investment); hedl.toml optional [project] section (project_number + field mappings); gate writes Project field updates on Issue state change; read-direction deferred; gh GraphQL subcommand avoids stdlib violation. Defer (B)(C)(D) explicitly.
acceptance_criteria: ADR phase: Proposed ADR-NNNN drafted with prior-art audit (GitHub native automation, Linear/Jira, existing Projects integrations + others); a concrete adopter scenario worked through ('4-person team using Projects to plan — how does Hedl interact?'); existential-challenger pass; operator decision: accept (A), accept (B/C), reject (D), or defer.
If accepted: hedl.toml [project] schema extension drafted; GraphQL query set drafted; sync model (eventual/real-time/gate-time) decided.
If deferred: docs/alternatives.md gains a 'GitHub Projects integration — considered, deferred, conditions to revisit' entry.
am_i_done.py passes (ADR PR is docs-only).

POST-ADR IMPLEMENTATION (if accepted): hedl.toml [project] parsed/validated; gh GraphQL helper (skill/hedl/scripts/github_projects.py, stdlib-only, shells to gh); install.py --doctor reports Project connectivity when configured; opt-in field-mapping migration for Issues-only adopters; integration test (fixture Project+Issue; Hedl closes Issue; Project status moves to Done within one am_i_done run); ADR-018 multi-operator race worked through; docs/upgrading.md + getting-started gain a 'Connecting a GitHub Project' section. Does NOT supersede the github-issues backend (Projects layers on).

backlog.title: feat(arch): extend declared-schema validation to generated JSON/TOML/YAML state, config, and the tier manifest
backlog.description:
ADR-CLASS (Proposed ADR before implementation). MORATORIUM CAVEAT: operator decides land-now vs defer-to-/phase-complete.

ROOT: Hedl validates markdown (agents/commands/ADRs/reviews) against markdown-schemas.json — strong determinism on LLM-authored content. But the JSON/TOML/YAML files Hedl ITSELF authors are read imperatively (json.load/tomllib.load, catch KeyError, fall back); no declared schema validates them. Asymmetry: stricter rigor on LLM output than on programmatic output — inverted from ADR-003's intent (LLMs are the looser part and should be the constrained one). Also markdown-schemas.json is not itself schema-validated (bootstrap hole); a script writing invalid state (e.g. a renamed field) crashes downstream with opaque KeyError, not 'schema violation at work.json field X required'; projected workflow YAML errors surface post-push (CI round-trip) that Hedl could catch pre-push.

IN SCOPE TO DECIDE: extend declared-schema validation to (1) JSON state (context/work/session/phases*.json); (2) JSON config (dispatch-rules, project-registry, markdown-schemas — validate the schema); (3) tiers.json; (4) hedl.toml; (5) workflow YAML (GitHub Actions schema); (6) PR body (already partly check_pr_template.py). Options: (A) home-rolled JSON validator extending check_markdown_schema.py (stdlib-only, JSON-Schema-subset syntax); (B) adopt jsonschema as a dev dep (raises the stdlib-only-contract question for am_i_done.py — test_stdlib_only.py); (C) hybrid — home-rolled for Hedl-owned formats, jsonschema(dev) for YAML vs the GitHub Actions schema; (D) don't extend (argue validation-by-use; risks contradicting ADR-003); (E) defer YAML/TOML, do JSON only (lowest scope, highest-frequency failure surface).

EXISTENTIAL CHALLENGE (ADR-017): prior art — jsonschema, actionlint (validates Actions YAML far better than a generic schema; Hedl could call it from the gate), taplo (TOML+schema), IDE schema stores; Hedl could VENDOR schemas from these rather than author. Challenge: (1) prior art; (2) are state files deterministic-by-construction (script is the schema) so schemas mainly catch migration drift the install.py migration registry may already cover; (3) does this push Hedl from 'workflow scaffold' toward 'config-driven framework' (maintenance cost grows with surface); (4) cost*frequency per file type (state files likely win; YAML likely loses to actionlint); (5) bootstrap — markdown-schemas.json needs its own meta-schema (land regardless).

RECOMMENDATION (subject to challenge): (C)+(E) scoping — this round home-rolled JSON validator for state files + the three config files + tiers.json; defer hedl.toml; defer YAML to a follow-up adopting actionlint via the gate subprocess pattern (dev-tier); bootstrap fix: markdown-schemas.json gets a declared meta-schema.
acceptance_criteria: ADR phase: Proposed ADR-NNNN drafted with prior-art audit (jsonschema, actionlint, taplo, IDE schema stores); existential-challenger pass; operator decision: A/B/C/D/E/other.
If accepted: schema files live in skill/hedl/schemas/ (one per declared shape); valid+invalid test fixtures per schema; the gate's `schemas` check expands to load JSON schemas.
If deferred: docs/alternatives.md gains an entry; ADR-003 docs note the partial scope honestly.
am_i_done.py passes (ADR PR is docs-only).

POST-ADR IMPLEMENTATION (if accepted): JSON schemas authored for in-scope files; check_markdown_schema.py grows a sibling check_json_schema.py (or is generalized); the gate `schemas` check covers markdown AND JSON with separate counts; per-schema test (valid passes, broken fails with line/field message); install.py --doctor reports JSON schema health; install.py --migrate flags .work/ files failing the new schema with repair steps; docs/upgrading.md gains a declared-schema section; bootstrap fix — markdown-schemas.json validates against its own meta-schema. Does NOT supersede markdown schema validation; extends it.

backlog.title: feat(arch): operator-reputation tracking — score clarification options, capture selections, modulate autonomy (project-local)
backlog.description:
ADR-CLASS (Proposed ADR before implementation). MORATORIUM CAVEAT: operator decides land-now vs defer-to-/phase-complete. Idea (operator, 2026-05-29): every time Hedl asks for clarification it presents OPTIONS, each scored 0-10 by Hedl's confidence. Each operator selection (or rejection-of-all) is captured as an insights event. Aggregate into a reputation score for Hedl's recommendations; as reputation rises, Hedl acts more autonomously (fewer clarification stops), having learned the operator's decision criteria.

HARD DESIGN CONSTRAINT (operator): this is PROJECT state, not Hedl state. Reputation starts fresh per project so it captures the consumer/adopter's preferences, NEVER the Hedl creator's. It lives in .work/insights/ (per-project, local-only — gitignored per WORK-0042), is created fresh on install, and is never shipped in skill/hedl/ defaults.

WHY ADR-CLASS — the autonomy-modulation half is in direct tension with Hedl's core: ADR-003 (deterministic over inference — the gate must not use inference) and ADR-025 (the operator's clarification/merge stop is the deliberate human checkpoint, structurally enforced). Auto-loosening checkpoints on a learned score puts inference into the control flow that decides WHEN to involve the human — the opposite of the deterministic gate Hedl is built on. The capture+scoring half is comparatively benign (it extends the existing AskUserQuestion + record_insights + /reflect pipeline); the 'become more autonomous' half is the load-bearing, safety-relevant risk.

EXISTENTIAL CHALLENGE (ADR-017; existential-challenger now a named mandatory reviewer per WORK-0005): (1) Does 'increase autonomy as reputation rises' violate ADR-003 + ADR-025? Likely acceptable only if autonomy-modulation is a TRANSPARENT DETERMINISTIC RULE with operator-set thresholds (e.g. 'after N consecutive accepted decisions of the same change_class, stop asking for that class'), NOT a learned/black-box score; reputation may INFORM but must never silently bypass a checkpoint. (2) LLM self-reported confidence (the 0-10) is uncalibrated — acting on it is dangerous; the real value is MEASURING calibration (does the score predict acceptance? a Brier-style metric), computed deterministically. (3) Whose reputation under multi-operator (ADR-018)? per-operator vs per-project aggregate — an average may erase differing criteria. (4) Safety/blast radius (CLAUDE.md): growing autonomy is hard to bound; HIGH-blast-radius decisions (main, deploys, external systems) must ALWAYS require human approval regardless of reputation. (5) Does the existing substrate already suffice — /iterate supervised vs autonomous + record_insights + /reflect (ADR-026)? The minimal version may be: record option-scores + selections, surface a calibration metric, let the operator MANUALLY widen autonomy — no auto-modulation. Prior art: active-learning/bandits, RLHF preference capture, trust-tier automation, calibration (Brier/ECE).
acceptance_criteria: ADR phase: Proposed ADR-NNNN with prior-art audit (active-learning/bandits, RLHF preference capture, trust-tier automation, calibration metrics) + existential-challenger pass.

The ADR MUST resolve the ADR-003/ADR-025 tension explicitly: autonomy-modulation is a deterministic operator-set rule (preferred) or a learned model (challenged hard); reputation INFORMS; high-blast-radius decisions always stop for a human regardless of score.
Decides whose-reputation under multi-operator (ADR-018): per-operator or per-project aggregate.
Confirms project-state-not-Hedl-state: data in .work/insights/, per-project, fresh on install, gitignored (WORK-0042), never in skill/hedl/ defaults.
Operator decision: accept (which variant — likely capture+calibration-metric + operator-driven autonomy, deferring auto-modulation), reject, or defer.
If deferred: docs/alternatives.md entry with conditions to revisit.
am_i_done.py passes (ADR PR is docs-only).

POST-ADR IMPLEMENTATION (if accepted): AskUserQuestion options carry a 0-10 confidence; {question, options+scores, selection-or-reject-all} recorded to .work/insights/ (project-local, gitignored); /reflect aggregates a deterministic calibration/reputation metric (pure arithmetic, ADR-003); any autonomy change is a transparent deterministic rule with operator-set thresholds + a hard high-blast-radius stop; per-project + fresh-per-install + never-shipped-in-defaults; tests + docs.

completed.title: fix: .github/ files parsed by GitHub must be real files, not symlinks
completed.description: GitHub does not follow symlinks for files it parses directly (workflows, dependabot, PR template, CODEOWNERS). install.py currently symlinks all these paths from skill/hedl/ projections, so the CI gate has never actually run in CI — all 'runs' since PR #2 are event:push fallback failures with zero check_runs created (verified via `gh api repos/:owner/:repo/commits/<sha>/check-runs` on multiple PR head SHAs: 218d3729, 452a2848, 5c6df42, 97b24a3). The UI label 'This run likely failed because of a workflow file issue' is GitHub rejecting the symlink-text as an invalid workflow file. This invalidates the 'PR-side CI gate' assumption that ADR-014 (Security/quality friction-aware) and the required-status-checks plan in WORK-0029 rest on. Discovered while investigating WORK-0029's acceptance-criterion-4 follow-up — the WORK-0029 investigation note's first hypothesis (no `required_status_checks` on main) is correct as written for the time, but is downstream of this root cause: even if `required_status_checks` had been configured, GitHub never had a context to attach because the workflow never published one.
acceptance_criteria: install.py distinguishes 'GitHub-parsed' .github paths from 'workflow-invoked' .github paths. For GitHub-parsed paths (workflows/*, dependabot.yml, PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md, CODEOWNERS), it COPIES the file content rather than symlinks.
install.py --doctor detects drift between the projected copy and the canonical source in skill/hedl/workflows/ (and equivalents) and fails with a clear remedy.
Existing symlinked .github files in this repo are replaced by real copies via install.py re-run (or a dedicated migration step).
After the fix, `gh api repos/neilgfoster/hedl/commits/<sha>/check-runs` shows real check_runs for the matrix names (am_i_done (3.11) through am_i_done (3.14)) on at least one PR head.
A corrected investigation note in .work/insights/WORK-0030-<topic>.md supersedes the WORK-0029 note's hypothesis with the actual root cause and links back to it.
am_i_done.py passes.
followups: After WORK-0030 lands and check_runs are confirmed via `gh api`, apply branch protection on main: required_status_checks with contexts ['am_i_done (3.11)', 'am_i_done (3.12)', 'am_i_done (3.13)', 'am_i_done (3.14)'] and strict: true.

completed.title: fix: am_i_done.py [verify] cmd allow-list / safe execution (RCE)
completed.description:
Repo-health 2026-05-28 (BLOCKING #6c, security-auditor) — `am_i_done.py:169-203` runs arbitrary `[verify.<name>] cmd` strings from `hedl.toml` with no allow-list. Any PR that adds a `[verify.x] cmd = "…"` becomes an RCE on the next gate run (locally and in CI). Constrain execution: require `cmd` be a list (not a shell string), execute without `shell=True`, restrict the executable to a configured allow-list of binaries (e.g., `pytest`, `mypy`, `ruff`, `npm`, `pnpm`, `make`, plus an opt-in list from hedl.toml), and document the contract in docs/versioning.md and tiers.md.

AUDIT-FIRST per WORK-0019 precedent: before fixing, audit the actual code path. am_i_done.py:169-203 (_run_declared_check) may already be safe — the prior external review noted shlex.split + list-form subprocess (shell=False) are already used, and shutil.which gates the executable, so the only real gap may be the executable allow-list (or the finding may be partially/fully wrong). If audit confirms safe behaviour, apply the WORK-0019 resolution pattern: no code change, but add a regression-guard test plus a negative meta-test that proves the test would catch the vulnerable form, plus an evidence note in .work/insights/WORK-0021-<topic>.md. If audit confirms a real vulnerability (e.g. a missing allow-list that permits an arbitrary executable), fix it with adversarial-reviewed code.
acceptance_criteria: Audit performed before any code change: trace the actual reachable inputs to am_i_done.py's [verify] cmd execution path (am_i_done.py:169-203, _run_declared_check), document findings in .work/insights/WORK-0021-<topic>.md. Decide fix-vs-guard explicitly.
am_i_done.py rejects hedl.toml `[verify.<name>]` entries that use shell strings, shell metacharacters, or executables outside the allow-list.
subprocess invocation uses an argv list and `shell=False`.
Default allow-list is documented; operator extension path is documented and itself constrained (e.g., still no shell expansion).
Tests cover: shell metacharacter rejection, disallowed executable rejection, allow-list extension, and a happy-path verify invocation.
am_i_done.py passes.
If resolved as false-positive: mirror the WORK-0019 resolution pattern (test_*.py with negative meta-test + insights note); change_class becomes chore.

completed.title: fix: budget_manager does not create .work/ in gate-only tier
completed.description: skill/hedl/scripts/budget_manager.py unconditionally calls `path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)` at lines ~118 and ~150. Gate-only adopters have no `.work/` by design — they install `--tier gate` precisely because they do not want the phase-tracking machinery. A budget operation silently creating `.work/` violates the tier contract and may confuse adopters into believing their tier is mis-configured. Invisible to a self-hosted /repo-health because this repo is team-tier and `.work/` exists from install. Fix: budget operations must detect the tier and either skip work-tracking writes on gate-only, route to a different location, or no-op cleanly. The exact resolution is part of the implementation. (Cross-link: alternatives.md flags budget_manager as a culling-candidate; if the budget tier mechanism is retired during Phase 1 the gate-only path is automatically resolved.)
